instruction: Why does Dimir Tempo play Orcish Bowmasters?
output: Orcish Bowmasters punishes every cantrip the opponent casts, creating a 1/1 army token and pinging a target for each card drawn beyond the first. In a format where 43% of decks play Brainstorm and 38% play Ponder, Bowmasters generates massive incidental value. It also has flash, making it a surprise blocker or end-of-turn threat that dodges sorcery-speed removal.

instruction: Why does Dimir Tempo play Thoughtseize over other discard?
output: Thoughtseize hits any nonland card for just one mana and one life, making it the most versatile hand disruption in Legacy. Unlike Hymn to Tourach (random) or Duress (noncreature only), Thoughtseize lets you surgically remove the opponent's best card regardless of type. The life cost is minimal in a tempo deck that plans to win before life totals matter.

instruction: Why does Dimir Tempo play Daze?
output: Daze is a free counterspell that lets you deploy threats while holding up interaction. In Dimir Tempo, you might play a turn-1 Delver or turn-2 Murktide and still counter the opponent's response by returning an Island. The tempo gain is enormous early game, and paired with Wasteland, it often catches opponents who can't afford to pay the extra mana.

instruction: Why does Dimir Tempo play Murktide Regent?
output: Murktide Regent is often a 6/6 or larger flyer for just UU after delving away graveyard cards. It dodges Lightning Bolt, demands premium removal like Swords to Plowshares, and closes games in 2-3 attacks. The delve cost is easy to fuel in a deck full of cantrips and fetchlands that naturally fill the graveyard.

instruction: Why does Sneak and Show play Lotus Petal?
output: Lotus Petal enables turn-1 or turn-2 Show and Tell by providing free mana acceleration. With Petal, the deck can cast Show and Tell as early as turn 1 (land + Petal + Ancient Tomb) or reliably on turn 2 through Daze. It also helps cast Sneak Attack on curve or protect the combo with Force of Will while tapped out.

instruction: Why does Sneak and Show play both Emrakul and Atraxa?
output: Emrakul, the Aeons Torn is the best Sneak Attack target because it gets an extra turn on cast and has annihilator 6, often winning in one attack. Atraxa, Grand Unifier is the best Show and Tell target because her ETB draws 7+ cards, providing overwhelming card advantage even if she is immediately answered. Having both gives the deck flexibility to choose the right threat for each situation.

instruction: Why does Sneak and Show play Force of Will?
output: Force of Will protects the combo turn. When you tap out for Show and Tell or Sneak Attack, you need free countermagic to stop the opponent's Force of Will or other disruption. Without Force backup, the combo is too fragile against the 45% of decks that play their own Force of Will.

instruction: Why does Lands play Exploration?
output: Exploration allows playing two lands per turn for just one green mana. This enables devastating plays like turn-1 Exploration into two lands, then turn-2 Wasteland plus recur with Life from the Loam to destroy two opponent lands per turn. It also accelerates the Dark Depths combo by getting both Depths and Thespian's Stage into play faster.

instruction: Why does Lands play Life from the Loam?
output: Life from the Loam is the engine that makes the entire Lands strategy work. Its dredge 3 fills the graveyard with lands that can be returned to hand, and it recurs Wasteland for repeated mana denial. Combined with Exploration, it creates a soft lock where you destroy an opponent's land every turn while never running out of your own.

instruction: Why does Lands play The Tabernacle at Pendrell Vale?
output: Tabernacle forces every creature to pay 1 mana during its controller's upkeep or be destroyed. Against creature-based decks like Elves, Goblins, or Death and Taxes, this is devastating because they cannot afford to keep their entire board alive. Lands can tutor for it with Crop Rotation and does not care about the upkeep cost since it runs very few creatures.

instruction: Why does The EPIC Storm play Burning Wish?
output: Burning Wish tutors for any sorcery from the sideboard, giving TES enormous flexibility. The sideboard contains situational win conditions (Tendrils of Agony, Empty the Warrens), removal (Pulverize), card draw (Past in Flames), and hate answers. This toolbox approach makes TES more resilient than other Storm variants because it can adapt mid-combo.

instruction: Why does Storm play Lion's Eye Diamond?
output: Lion's Eye Diamond provides 3 mana of any color for free by discarding your hand. In Storm, you cast it alongside Infernal Tutor (which benefits from an empty hand via hellbent) or crack it in response to Burning Wish. The 3 free mana dramatically increases storm count and total mana available for the kill turn.

instruction: Why does Death and Taxes play Aether Vial?
output: Aether Vial lets D&T put creatures into play at instant speed without paying mana, bypassing both Daze and Thalia's own tax on noncreature spells. It effectively gives every creature flash, enabling surprise blocks with Flickerwisp or end-of-turn Stoneforge Mystic. It also frees up mana for Rishadan Port and Wasteland activations.

instruction: Why does Death and Taxes play Thalia, Guardian of Thraben?
output: Thalia makes every noncreature spell cost 1 more, which is devastating in Legacy where most spells cost 0-2 mana. Brainstorm becomes 2 mana, Force of Will's alternative cost adds 1, and combo decks need extra mana for every ritual and cantrip. She is a 2/1 first striker that attacks well and demands immediate removal.

instruction: Why does Death and Taxes play Rishadan Port?
output: Rishadan Port taps an opponent's land during their upkeep, effectively keeping them one mana behind every turn. Combined with Wasteland and Thalia, this creates a crushing mana denial package that prevents opponents from casting their spells on curve. D&T can afford this because Aether Vial provides mana-free creature deployment.

instruction: Why does Painter play Goblin Welder?
output: Goblin Welder swaps artifacts between the graveyard and battlefield at instant speed. If Grindstone or Painter's Servant is destroyed, Welder can bring it back immediately by sacrificing a cheap artifact. It also enables value plays like recurring Ichor Wellspring for card draw.

instruction: Why does Painter play Red Elemental Blast effects?
output: When Painter's Servant is in play naming blue, every permanent and spell becomes blue. This means Red Elemental Blast and Pyroblast can counter ANY spell and destroy ANY permanent for just one red mana, transforming narrow sideboard cards into unconditional interaction.

instruction: Why does Oops All Spells play zero lands?
output: The entire strategy depends on Balustrade Spy or Undercity Informer milling your complete library when it targets you. If there were any lands in the deck, the mill would stop at the first land. With zero lands, your entire library goes to the graveyard, triggering Narcomoeba, enabling Dread Return, and winning with Thassa's Oracle.

instruction: Why does Oops All Spells play Force of Will?
output: Despite being a combo deck, Oops plays Force of Will because it needs protection for its combo turn. The deck is extremely vulnerable to a single counterspell or Surgical Extraction, so free countermagic is essential.

instruction: Why does Eldrazi play Chalice of the Void?
output: Chalice on 1 counters the majority of Legacy's most played spells: Brainstorm, Ponder, Swords to Plowshares, Lightning Bolt, Thoughtseize, Daze, and many more. Eldrazi can deploy Chalice on turn 1 via Ancient Tomb, and since Eldrazi creatures cost 2+ mana, the Chalice does not affect your own spells.

instruction: Why does Doomsday play Thassa's Oracle as its win condition?
output: After resolving Doomsday (which leaves a 5-card library), you draw through the pile and cast Oracle with 0-1 cards remaining. Oracle wins on ETB when devotion to blue meets or exceeds cards in library. It is a single compact win condition that cannot be stopped by creature removal once the trigger is on the stack.

instruction: Why is Dimir Tempo favored against Storm?
output: Dimir Tempo has the exact disruption package Storm hates: Thoughtseize strips key combo pieces before the combo turn, Force of Will and Daze counter critical spells during the combo turn, and Wasteland can deny the mana Storm needs. The tempo clock means Storm cannot take many turns to rebuild after disruption.

instruction: Why does Lands struggle against Oops All Spells?
output: Lands has almost no instant-speed interaction that can stop the Oops combo. Wasteland, Maze of Ith, and Punishing Fire are all irrelevant against a deck that wins by milling itself and casting Thassa's Oracle. Lands needs to rely entirely on sideboard graveyard hate, which is insufficient against Force of Will backup.

instruction: Why is Death and Taxes good against fair blue decks?
output: Thalia taxes every cantrip and counterspell, Rishadan Port and Wasteland deny mana, and Aether Vial bypasses countermagic entirely. The cumulative tax effect means blue decks can rarely operate at full efficiency. Flickerwisp and Skyclave Apparition handle problematic permanents.

instruction: Why is Sneak and Show favored against Lands?
output: Lands does not play counterspells, so Show and Tell resolves easily. Once Emrakul enters play, Lands has no removal that handles protection from colored spells. Maze of Ith prevents damage but not the annihilator trigger, so Lands still loses permanents every turn.

instruction: Why does Burn struggle in the current Legacy metagame?
output: Orcish Bowmasters in 29% of decks punishes Burn's reliance on drawing cards. Force of Will counters critical burn spells for free. Many current decks gain incidental life or run enough interaction to survive. Burn also cannot beat combo decks that win before it deals 20 damage.

instruction: Why is Painter favored against combo decks?
output: Painter has a fast combo of its own that can win on turn 2-3, racing most combo decks. It also plays Chalice of the Void and Red Elemental Blast effects that become unconditional counters when Painter's Servant is in play.

instruction: Why does Eldrazi struggle against Sneak and Show?
output: Eldrazi's primary disruption is Chalice on 1, but Show and Tell costs 3 and Sneak Attack costs 4 so Chalice does not help. Thought-Knot Seer can strip a key card, but Sneak and Show has enough cantrips to recover. Eldrazi creatures cannot race an Emrakul.

instruction: Why is the Dimir Tempo vs Ocelot Pride matchup close?
output: Dimir has Thoughtseize and Bowmasters which are effective against creatures, but Ocelot generates tokens and energy that overwhelm one-for-one removal. Ocelot does not rely on cantrips so Bowmasters draw-punishing ability is less relevant. The matchup often comes down to whether Dimir sticks an early Murktide and races.

instruction: Should I play Lightning Bolt or Fatal Push in Izzet Delver?
output: Lightning Bolt is generally better because it deals 3 damage to any target including the opponent. This reach is critical for closing games. Fatal Push is better against Murktide Regent which dodges Bolt, but cannot go face. Most lists play 4 Bolt with 0-2 Push.

instruction: How many Force of Will should I play?
output: Almost always 4 copies. Force of Will is the format safety valve against unfair decks, and you need to see it consistently against combo. You need at least 19 blue cards in your deck, preferably 23-24, to reliably pitch to Force.

instruction: When should I cut Daze from my deck?
output: Daze is bad in pure control decks that want to hit every land drop. It is also weak in grindy post-board matchups where opponents have plenty of mana. Most Delver players sideboard out 1-2 Dazes against midrange and control but keep all 4 against combo.

instruction: When is Pyroblast better than Red Elemental Blast?
output: They are functionally identical in almost all situations. Pyroblast can target non-blue things (fizzling on resolution), while REB cannot target non-blue at all. This matters with Chalice on 1 or Meddling Mage. Most players split 2-2.

instruction: Should I play Surgical Extraction or Leyline of the Void?
output: Leyline is better as a hard lock against dedicated graveyard decks since it prevents cards from entering the graveyard entirely. Surgical is better as a reactive answer that removes specific combo pieces while leaving your own graveyard intact for Murktide. Choose based on how much graveyard combo you expect and whether your deck uses its own graveyard.

instruction: What makes a good Legacy sideboard?
output: A good Legacy sideboard covers the top 5-8 matchups with 2-3 cards each. Include graveyard hate, artifact and enchantment removal, color-specific answers like Pyroblast, anti-combo cards like Flusterstorm, and creature removal like Toxic Deluge. Avoid narrow one-ofs.

instruction: How many cantrips should a blue Legacy deck play?
output: Most blue Legacy decks play 8 cantrips: 4 Brainstorm and 4 Ponder. This provides the consistency needed in a format with diverse matchups. Some decks add Preordain as 9-10. Control decks sometimes play fewer to fit more answers.

instruction: How many dual lands does Dimir Tempo need?
output: Typically 3-4 Underground Sea. You need enough blue and black sources for Brainstorm, Force of Will, Thoughtseize, and Bowmasters reliably. Three Underground Sea plus fetchlands gives around 10+ sources of each color. Some builds run 4 for Wasteland resilience.

instruction: How many basic lands should I play in Legacy?
output: Most Legacy decks play 2-4 basic lands to protect against Wasteland and Blood Moon. Tempo decks typically play 2, control decks play 3-5. Running zero basics is risky when 48% of decks play Wasteland.

instruction: When should I fetch a basic land instead of a dual?
output: Fetch a basic when you expect Wasteland, when you do not need the second color this turn, or when at low life against aggro. Against Blood Moon decks, get basics in play before Moon resolves. When in doubt, fetch basic Island first.

instruction: Why do Legacy decks play so many fetchlands?
output: Fetchlands fix mana by finding any dual land type, shuffle away bad cards after Brainstorm, fill the graveyard for Murktide and delve, and thin the deck slightly. A Polluted Delta can find Underground Sea, Volcanic Island, Tropical Island, Tundra, or basic lands.

instruction: How does Mox Diamond work in Lands?
output: Mox Diamond enters play by discarding a land card, then taps for any color. In Lands this is pure upside because discarded lands return with Life from the Loam. It enables explosive turn-1 plays like Exploration plus two land drops.

instruction: What should I sideboard against Reanimator?
output: Bring in all graveyard hate: Leyline of the Void, Surgical Extraction, or Endurance. Leyline is best because it comes down before the game starts. Also keep counterspells since Force of Will and Daze are critical for stopping the reanimate spell itself.

instruction: When do I sideboard out Force of Will?
output: Board out Force in grindy fair matchups where the 2-for-1 card disadvantage is too costly. Against midrange decks, every card matters and pitching to counter a creature is a bad trade. Keep Force against any deck that can win with a single spell.

instruction: How many sideboard slots should I dedicate to combo hate?
output: In the current meta, 4-6 slots is typical. With combo at about 40% of the metagame, you need enough to improve multiple matchups. A common setup is 3-4 Leyline of the Void plus 2 Flusterstorm.

instruction: What is the best sideboard card in Legacy right now?
output: Leyline of the Void at 36% of decks is the most played sideboard card. It shuts down Reanimator, Dredge, Oops All Spells, and weakens Doomsday without costing any mana when in your opening hand.

instruction: Should I play Endurance or Leyline of the Void?
output: Leyline is a hard lock against dedicated graveyard decks. Endurance is a flexible card that is also a 3/4 body and works with Green Sun's Zenith. Green-based fair decks prefer Endurance; blue decks needing a hard shutdown prefer Leyline.

instruction: How do I sideboard against a diverse Legacy metagame?
output: Focus on the top 5-8 decks by metagame share rather than covering everything. Have a plan for Dimir Tempo, Ocelot Pride, Oops/combo, and Sneak and Show. Accept weak matchups against fringe decks since the math favors being heavily favored against common opponents.

instruction: Why does Beanstalk Control play Up the Beanstalk?
output: Up the Beanstalk is a 2-mana enchantment that draws a card whenever you cast a spell with mana value 5 or greater. In Legacy, Force of Will (MV 5), Solitude (MV 5), Fury (MV 5), and Subtlety (MV 5) all trigger it even when cast for their free alternative costs. This turns pitch spells into cantrips, generating absurd card advantage while doing things you already wanted to do.

instruction: Why does Beanstalk Control play Solitude?
output: Solitude is a free removal spell that exiles a creature by pitching a white card. In Beanstalk, it also triggers Up the Beanstalk to draw a card, making the pitch cost less painful. The 3/2 lifelink body is relevant when evoked and blinked with Ephemerate, gaining 3 life and exiling another creature. It handles problematic threats like Murktide Regent and True-Name Nemesis that dodge other removal.

instruction: Why does Reanimator play Reanimate over other reanimation spells?
output: Reanimate costs just 1 black mana, making it the cheapest and fastest reanimation spell. The life cost equal to the target creature's mana value is steep (8 for Griselbrand, 7 for Archon of Cruelty) but irrelevant when the reanimated creature immediately takes over the game. At 1 mana, Reanimate can be cast through Daze and Thalia while still leaving mana for protection.

instruction: Why does Reanimator play Archon of Cruelty?
output: Archon of Cruelty has an ETB that forces the opponent to sacrifice a creature or planeswalker, discard a card, and lose 3 life, while you draw a card and gain 3 life. This 6-for-1 swing on entry is devastating even if Archon is immediately removed. Unlike Griselbrand, Archon cannot be exiled by Karakas (it is not legendary) and provides value regardless of your life total.

instruction: Why did Reanimator decline after the Entomb ban?
output: Entomb was a 1-mana instant that put any creature directly into the graveyard, enabling turn-1 Entomb into turn-1 Reanimate kills. Without it, Reanimator must use slower discard outlets like Faithful Mending or Careful Study, which cost 2 mana and require drawing the creature first. This extra turn of setup gives opponents time to find Surgical Extraction, Force of Will, or graveyard hate.

instruction: Why does Elves play Glimpse of Nature?
output: Glimpse of Nature turns every creature cast into a cantrip for the rest of the turn, drawing a card per creature. With Heritage Druid providing mana from untapped elves and Nettle Sentinel untapping on each green spell, Elves can chain 5-10 creatures in a single turn, drawing into Natural Order or Craterhoof Behemoth for the kill. Glimpse is the engine that turns a pile of 1-mana creatures into a combo deck.

instruction: Why does Elves play Natural Order?
output: Natural Order sacrifices a green creature to tutor any green creature directly into play. In Elves, this means sacrificing a 1-mana elf to get Craterhoof Behemoth, which gives all your creatures +X/+X and trample where X is your creature count. With 5+ elves in play, this is typically a one-shot kill. Natural Order also finds silver bullets like Endurance or Collector Ouphe from the toolbox.

instruction: Why does Dredge play Lion's Eye Diamond?
output: LED is the best card in Dredge because discarding your hand is an UPSIDE - it puts dredge cards into the graveyard where you want them. Cracking LED in response to a draw effect lets you dredge on the next draw while also generating 3 mana for Faithless Looting flashback or other spells. Turn-1 LED into draw spell is the explosive start that makes Dredge competitive.

instruction: Why does Dredge play Ichorid?
output: Ichorid returns from the graveyard to the battlefield for free at the beginning of your upkeep by exiling a black creature card. Since Dredge fills the graveyard with creatures naturally, Ichorid provides a recurring threat that costs zero mana and zero cards from hand. Multiple Ichorids coming back every turn create inevitability even through removal.

instruction: Why does Red Stompy play Blood Moon?
output: Blood Moon turns all nonbasic lands into Mountains, shutting off dual lands, fetchlands, Wasteland, Urza's Saga, Dark Depths, and utility lands. In a format where most decks run 15+ nonbasics, Blood Moon effectively locks opponents out of casting their spells. Red Stompy only needs Mountains, so Blood Moon does not affect its own mana base at all.

instruction: Why does Red Stompy play The One Ring?
output: The One Ring provides protection from everything for a turn (buying time to set up locks) and draws increasing numbers of cards each turn. In a prison deck that aims to stop the opponent from playing, The One Ring provides the card advantage needed to find more lock pieces and threats. The cumulative life loss is acceptable because the opponent should be locked out by then.

instruction: Why does Red Stompy play Chrome Mox?
output: Chrome Mox provides turn-1 acceleration by imprinting a nonartifact, nonland card. Combined with Ancient Tomb or City of Traitors, Chrome Mox enables turn-1 Blood Moon, turn-1 Chalice of the Void on 1, or turn-1 Trinisphere. The card disadvantage of imprinting is acceptable because a resolved turn-1 lock piece often wins the game by itself.

instruction: Why does Aluren play Acererak the Archlich?
output: Acererak the Archlich costs 3 mana, so it can be cast for free with Aluren. When Acererak enters the battlefield, you choose to not complete the dungeon, which returns Acererak to your hand. This creates an infinite loop: cast Acererak for free, fail dungeon, return to hand, repeat. Each iteration progresses you through a dungeon, eventually dealing lethal damage through Lost Mine of Phandelver's drain room.

instruction: Why does Cloudpost Ramp play Glimmerpost?
output: Glimmerpost gains 1 life for each Locus you control when it enters, but more importantly it is a Locus that makes Cloudpost tap for more mana. With Cloudpost tapping for mana equal to Loci count, each Glimmerpost effectively adds 1 mana to every Cloudpost. Two Cloudposts and a Glimmerpost means each Cloudpost taps for 3 mana - 6 total from two lands.

instruction: Why does Stiflenought play Phyrexian Dreadnought?
output: Phyrexian Dreadnought is a 12/12 trample for just 1 colorless mana, with a drawback that you must sacrifice 12 power of creatures when it enters or sacrifice it. With Stifle countering the ETB trigger, or Dress Down removing all abilities, you get a 12/12 on turn 1-2 for practically no cost. A 12/12 trample kills in two attacks and survives every damage-based removal in the format.

instruction: Why does Food Chain play Misthollow Griffin?
output: Misthollow Griffin can be cast from exile, which is unique in Magic. Food Chain exiles a creature to generate mana equal to its mana value plus 1 (but only for creature spells). Exile Griffin to Food Chain for 5 mana, recast Griffin from exile for 4 mana, net +1 mana each cycle. This creates infinite mana for creature spells, enabling Walking Ballista for infinite damage.

instruction: Why is Storm favored against Death and Taxes?
output: D&T has no free counterspells to stop the combo turn. Thalia taxes Storm's spells by 1 each, but Storm can remove Thalia with a bounce spell or simply generate enough mana to pay through the tax. D&T's clock is too slow to kill before Storm goes off on turns 3-4, and Rishadan Port tapping one land is insufficient disruption against a deck that generates 10+ mana in a single turn.

instruction: Why is Doomsday harder to play than other combo decks?
output: Doomsday requires you to construct a 5-card pile that wins through whatever disruption the opponent might have, accounting for cards in hand, mana available, and cards you need to draw through. Different matchups require different piles (play around Surgical, play around Force of Will, play around Stifle). A wrong pile construction means you lose with no recourse. Other combo decks just need to find pieces and cast them.

instruction: Why does Merfolk struggle in the current metagame?
output: Orcish Bowmasters in 29% of decks is devastating against a deck full of 2-toughness creatures. Bowmasters kills Lord of Atlantis on sight and punishes every cantrip Merfolk casts. The deck also lacks free countermagic beyond Force of Will, making it weaker against combo than Delver variants. True-Name Nemesis is the one bright spot but costs 3 mana in a deck that wants to curve out with lords.

instruction: Why is Ocelot Pride Midrange strong against creature decks?
output: Ocelot Pride creates tokens on every non-combat damage trigger, so Lightning Bolt, Bowmasters pings, and Guide of Souls drain all generate extra bodies. The deck goes wide faster than most creature strategies can manage, and Swords to Plowshares plus Solitude handle any individual large threat. Against decks like D&T or Elves, Ocelot simply out-values them in creature combat.

instruction: Why does Infect have polarized matchups?
output: Infect wins by dealing just 10 poison with pump spells on an unblocked infect creature, which can happen as early as turn 2. Against decks without removal (combo, some prison), this is virtually unbeatable speed. But against decks with efficient removal (Swords to Plowshares, Lightning Bolt, Orcish Bowmasters), killing the infect creature in response to a pump spell is a devastating blowout that wastes multiple cards.

instruction: How do you build a mana base for a three-color Legacy deck?
output: Start with 4 fetchlands that find both your primary and secondary duals (e.g., Polluted Delta in UBx). Run 3-4 of your primary dual land, 1-2 of your secondary dual, and 2-3 basics (at least one of each primary color). Add 4 Wasteland if your deck is tempo-oriented. Total lands typically 18-20 for tempo, 22-24 for midrange. The key is ensuring 14+ sources of your primary color for turn-1 plays and 10+ of each secondary color.

instruction: When should a Legacy deck play Ancient Tomb?
output: Ancient Tomb belongs in decks that benefit from 2 colorless mana on turn 1 and can tolerate the 2 life per tap. This means prison decks (Chalice, Trinisphere, Blood Moon), artifact combo (Mystic Forge, Painter), Eldrazi (creatures cost 2+), and Show and Tell variants. It does NOT belong in tempo decks that need colored mana for cantrips or control decks that need every life point. The 2 life per use adds up fast.

instruction: Why do some decks play City of Traitors over Ancient Tomb?
output: City of Traitors provides 2 colorless mana without the 2 life cost, which matters in Eldrazi and prison decks that activate it many times. The downside is that it is sacrificed when you play another land, so it is better in decks that want to deploy one big play early (turn-1 Chalice + creature) rather than sustained mana production. Many decks play a split of both to maximize explosive starts.

instruction: How many lands should a combo deck play?
output: Combo decks typically play 14-18 lands depending on their mana curve and acceleration. Storm plays 14-15 with rituals providing the rest. Sneak and Show plays 16-18 with Lotus Petal and Ancient Tomb. Oops plays 0 (by design). Doomsday plays 16-17 with Dark Ritual. The key is having enough mana to cast your combo pieces on turns 2-3 while maximizing spell density for consistency.

instruction: What is the correct fetchland configuration for Dimir Tempo?
output: Most Dimir Tempo lists play 8 fetchlands: 4 Polluted Delta (finds Underground Sea and both basics) plus 4 Misty Rainforest, Flooded Strand, or Scalding Tarn (any blue fetch that finds Island). The second set of fetches is chosen based on splash potential: Misty for green sideboard cards, Scalding Tarn for Volcanic Island splash, Flooded Strand for Tundra splash. All 8 find basic Island, which is the most important basic.

instruction: How should I sideboard against Eldrazi?
output: Chalice of the Void on 1 is their key card, so bring in cards that cost 0 or 2+ mana. Force of Vigor destroys Chalice for free. Engineered Explosives on 0 catches Chalice. Keep your big threats (Murktide survives anything Eldrazi throws at it). Cut low-impact 1-mana spells that get countered by Chalice. The matchup is about resolving threats through Chalice and going bigger than their creatures.

instruction: How should I sideboard against Lands?
output: Blood Moon effects are the best answer to Lands since their entire strategy is built on nonbasic lands. Surgical Extraction on Life from the Loam shuts down their recursion engine. Dress Down prevents Thespian's Stage from copying Dark Depths. Price of Progress deals massive damage against a deck with 30+ nonbasic lands. Cut your Dazes (they have few spells to counter) and creature removal (they have few creatures).

instruction: How should I sideboard against Ocelot Pride?
output: Bring in sweepers like Toxic Deluge (kills through Solitude protection) and Massacre (free against white). Engineered Explosives on 1 catches most of their threats. Keep Orcish Bowmasters (excellent against their token generation). Cut Daze (they have enough mana to pay) and narrow countermagic. The goal is to go bigger with card advantage and sweepers rather than trying to one-for-one their value engine.

instruction: How should I sideboard against Doomsday?
output: Keep all countermagic (Force, Daze, Flusterstorm). Bring in Surgical Extraction (exile Doomsday from graveyard after they cast it, or target Thassa's Oracle). Stifle the Oracle ETB trigger if you play it. Thoughtseize is excellent since stripping Doomsday itself prevents the combo. The key is preventing Doomsday from resolving or disrupting the pile execution afterward.

instruction: When should I bring in Mindbreak Trap?
output: Mindbreak Trap is best against Storm and Oops All Spells because it exiles spells for free when the opponent has cast 3+ spells this turn (which both decks do every combo turn). It cannot be countered by Flusterstorm since it is exiled rather than countered. Bring it in against any deck that chains multiple spells in a single turn. It is dead against fair decks that cast one spell per turn.

instruction: When should I bring in Null Rod or Collector Ouphe?
output: Null Rod and Collector Ouphe shut off all activated abilities of artifacts. Bring them in against Painter (stops Grindstone activation), 8-Cast and Affinity (stops Urza's Saga, artifact mana), Mystic Forge (stops the Forge itself), and any deck heavily reliant on artifact mana (Lion's Eye Diamond, Lotus Petal, Chrome Mox). Be careful not to board them in when your own deck uses artifact mana.

instruction: How do I use Force of Vigor effectively?
output: Force of Vigor destroys two artifacts and/or enchantments for free by pitching a green card. It is devastating against Chalice of the Void (on 1 and 2), Urza's Saga and its Construct tokens, Blood Moon, Ensnaring Bridge, and The One Ring. The two-for-one aspect makes it better than Nature's Claim in matchups where the opponent has multiple problematic artifacts or enchantments. Always pitch it rather than hard-casting if possible.

instruction: What is the 60-card rule in Legacy deck construction?
output: Legacy decks must have a minimum of 60 cards in the main deck and up to 15 in the sideboard. While you can play more than 60, virtually every competitive deck plays exactly 60 because more cards reduces consistency - you are less likely to draw your best cards. The only exception is Yorion companion decks that play 80 cards, accepting reduced consistency for the companion advantage.

instruction: How do I choose between a 60-card and 80-card build?
output: Play 80 cards only with Yorion, Sky Nomad as companion, and only if your deck has enough blink targets to justify Yorion's ETB. Death and Taxes (Yorion BW), Beanstalk Control (Yorion builds), and some Bant builds use 80 cards. The 20 extra cards reduce consistency but Yorion's guaranteed value compensates. If your deck does not have strong ETB creatures to blink, 60 cards is strictly better.

instruction: How do I decide which cards to play 4 copies of?
output: Play 4 copies of cards you want to see in every opening hand and every game. In Dimir Tempo, that means 4 Brainstorm, 4 Ponder, 4 Force of Will, 4 Daze, 4 Wasteland, 4 Bowmasters. Play 3 copies of cards that are great but occasionally redundant (3 Murktide, 3 Thoughtseize). Play 1-2 copies of situational cards or legends (1 Karakas, 2 Snuff Out). The cantrips help find your 1-2 ofs when needed.

instruction: What does a well-constructed Legacy deck curve look like?
output: Tempo decks peak at 1-2 mana: 20+ spells at 0-1 mana, 8-12 at 2 mana, 2-4 at 3+ mana. Control decks are flatter: 10-12 at 1 mana, 8-10 at 2, 4-6 at 3, 2-4 at 4+. Combo decks are bimodal: many 0-1 mana spells (rituals, cantrips) and a few high-impact 3-4 mana payoffs. Prison decks spike at 0 and 3: fast mana (0) into lock pieces (3). Legacy is defined by mana efficiency - 4+ mana spells need to be game-winning to justify their slot.

instruction: I like controlling the board and winning with inevitability. What Legacy deck should I play?
output: Beanstalk Control or Jeskai Control are your best options. Beanstalk generates card advantage through Up the Beanstalk triggers on pitch spells, plays sweepers and removal, and wins through accumulated value. Jeskai Control uses Swords to Plowshares, Lightning Bolt, countermagic, and planeswalkers to grind opponents out. Lands is another option if you want a non-traditional control deck that operates entirely through lands.

instruction: I like fast, aggressive decks that win before the opponent sets up. What Legacy deck should I play?
output: For combo speed: Oops All Spells can win turn 1, Storm and Doomsday win turns 2-3, Sneak and Show wins turns 2-3. For aggressive creature speed: Ocelot Pride Midrange deploys efficient threats from turn 1 and overwhelms quickly, Burn goes face from turn 1. For tempo aggression: Dimir Tempo and Izzet Delver deploy cheap threats and protect them with free countermagic while denying opponent's mana.

instruction: I want to play a deck that taxes and disrupts the opponent. What Legacy deck should I play?
output: Death and Taxes is the premier tax deck: Thalia taxes spells, Rishadan Port and Wasteland tax mana, and Aether Vial lets you deploy creatures while keeping tax mana open. Red Stompy provides artifact-based prison with Chalice of the Void, Blood Moon, and Trinisphere. Eldrazi combines Chalice prison with large aggressive creatures. All three specialize in preventing the opponent from playing their game.

instruction: I have a small budget. What competitive Legacy deck can I build?
output: Oops All Spells at $1100 is the cheapest tier 1 deck - zero dual lands needed. Burn at $1500 is a consistent performer with no expensive Reserved List cards. Belcher at $1200 is similar to Oops in strategy and price. Red Stompy at $2500 avoids dual lands and uses Ancient Tomb as its main investment. Eldrazi at $2100 is all colorless. All five can win tournaments and serve as entry points into Legacy.

instruction: I want to play a unique brew in Legacy. How do I approach it?
output: Start by identifying a meta weakness: if 14.6% of the field is Dimir Tempo, build something that beats UB. Study underplayed archetypes with high win rates (Aluren won the Showcase Qualifier at only 1.2% meta share). Use established Legacy shells as foundations (cantrip + Force of Will, Ancient Tomb + Chalice, Green Sun's Zenith toolbox) and add your unique angle. Test extensively against tier 1 decks before committing - a brew that loses to the top 3 decks is just a bad deck.

instruction: How many copies of Wasteland should I play?
output: 4 copies in tempo decks (Delver, D&T) where mana denial is core strategy. 4 in Lands where Wasteland recursion with Loam is the primary gameplan. 2-3 in midrange decks that want it but cannot afford too many colorless sources. 0 in combo decks that need every land to produce colored mana and cannot afford to sacrifice a land drop for mana denial. 0 in control decks that need to hit land drops every turn.

instruction: How many copies of Thoughtseize should I play?
output: 4 copies in Dimir Tempo where proactive disruption is essential. 3-4 in combo decks that need to clear the way for their combo turn. 2-3 in midrange decks as a flexible disruption spell. 0 in most blue control decks that prefer reactive answers (countermagic). The life cost matters against aggro, so some players trim to 2-3 post-board against Burn or Ocelot Pride. Thoughtseize is best when you know what to take, so format knowledge increases its value.

instruction: How many copies of Orcish Bowmasters should I play?
output: 4 copies in any black deck that can cast it reliably. Bowmasters is the most played creature in Legacy at 29% of decks because it punishes cantrips (43% of decks play Brainstorm), provides a flash threat, and creates tokens for board presence. The only reason to play fewer is if your deck cannot reliably produce BB by turn 2, or if your strategy does not benefit from creature-based interaction.

instruction: How many copies of Swords to Plowshares should I play?
output: 4 copies in creature-heavy matchups and control decks. 2-3 in tempo decks that also run other removal. 0 in combo decks that do not care about opposing creatures. Swords is the best creature removal spell ever printed at 1 mana instant exile, but the life gain for the opponent can be relevant against combo where every point of reach matters. Some players trim Swords against combo-heavy fields.

instruction: Why does Boros Initiative play Caves of Chaos Adventurer?
output: Caves of Chaos Adventurer provides the Initiative when it enters the battlefield, which advances through the Undercity dungeon. The dungeon rooms provide card advantage, removal, and eventually a 5/5 token. In a prison deck that slows the opponent with Chalice and Thalia, the Initiative provides incremental value every turn. The 5/3 body for 4 mana is reasonable, and Ancient Tomb enables turn-2 deployment.

instruction: Why does Boros Initiative play Solitude?
output: Solitude can be cast for free by pitching a white card, providing instant-speed removal at zero mana. In a deck without blue cantrips to find answers, having free removal is essential for handling surprise threats. Solitude also has lifelink on its 3/2 body, which helps stabilize after Ancient Tomb life loss. The pitch cost is acceptable because Boros Initiative runs many white cards.

instruction: Why does Dredge play Golgari Grave-Troll?
output: Golgari Grave-Troll has dredge 6, the highest dredge count in Legacy. Each time you would draw a card, you can instead mill 6 cards and return Grave-Troll to hand. Milling 6 cards fills the graveyard with Ichorid, Narcomoeba, Bridge from Below triggers, and more dredge cards. The more cards milled, the more resources generated. Grave-Troll's creature body is rarely cast but its dredge ability is the engine that makes the deck work.

instruction: Why does Dredge play Bridge from Below?
output: Bridge from Below creates a 2/2 zombie token whenever a creature you control dies while Bridge is in your graveyard. In Dredge, creatures die constantly (Ichorid sacrifices itself, Narcomoeba enables Dread Return by being sacrificed). Each death creates a zombie. However, Bridge is exiled if an opponent's creature dies, which is why Orcish Bowmasters (creating a 1/1 that can die in combat) is so devastating against Dredge.

instruction: Why does Omni-Tell play Omniscience instead of Sneak Attack?
output: Omniscience lets you cast ALL spells from your hand for free, not just creatures. After resolving Show and Tell for Omniscience, you can cast Emrakul (extra turn + annihilator 6), Atraxa (draw 10+ cards), Brainstorm (find more threats), and Force of Will (protect your board), all for free. Sneak Attack only puts creatures in play temporarily (sacrificed at end of turn). Omniscience keeps everything permanently and enables casting any card type.

instruction: Why does Mystic Forge Combo play Karn, the Great Creator?
output: Karn, the Great Creator has a -2 ability that gets any artifact from outside the game (sideboard) or from exile. This provides a toolbox of silver bullets: Ensnaring Bridge against creatures, Mycosynth Lattice to lock opponent out of mana with Karn's passive (opponents cannot activate abilities of artifacts), Tormod's Crypt for graveyard hate, Walking Ballista as a win condition. Karn also shuts off opponent's artifact abilities with his passive, disabling Aether Vial, Mox, and equipment.

instruction: Why does Coveted Jewel Combo play flicker effects?
output: Coveted Jewel is an artifact that taps for 3 mana of any color and draws 3 cards when it enters play. By flickering it (exiling and returning with Ephemerate or Yorion), you get the ETB again: draw 3 more cards and 3 more mana. Each flicker is a 3-card draw engine. Combined with the 3 mana it generates, flickering Jewel creates a self-sustaining combo that draws your entire deck and generates infinite mana. The kill is usually Thassa's Oracle or Walking Ballista.

instruction: Why does Hogaak play Altar of Dementia?
output: Altar of Dementia sacrifices a creature to mill that many cards from any player's library. Hogaak is an 8/8 that can be cast from the graveyard by convoking and delving. Mill Hogaak to Altar, mill 8 cards from your own library (filling graveyard with more creatures), recast Hogaak using convoke and delve, repeat. Each loop mills 8 cards and eventually mills your entire library for a Thassa's Oracle win, or mills the opponent's library directly. Altar provides both a combo finish and graveyard fueling.

instruction: Why is Painter favored against Death and Taxes?
output: Painter's two-card combo (Servant + Grindstone) is faster than D&T can deploy its hatebears. D&T has no counterspells to stop the combo. Thalia taxes Grindstone activation by 0 (it is an artifact ability, not a noncreature spell). Rishadan Port and Wasteland slow Painter slightly but Ancient Tomb dodges both (produces colorless). Red Elemental Blast with Painter naming blue destroys any permanent and counters any spell, overwhelming D&T's creature-based interaction.

instruction: Why is Oops All Spells favored against Lands?
output: Lands operates entirely at sorcery speed through land abilities. It has no counterspells, no instant-speed graveyard hate in the maindeck, and no way to interact with a combo that wins by milling a library and casting Thassa's Oracle. Lands' strengths (Wasteland, Maze of Ith, Punishing Fire) are all irrelevant against Oops. The only interaction is sideboard Bojuka Bog (found by Crop Rotation) or Surgical Extraction, which requires the Oops player to fill the graveyard first.

instruction: Why is Elves weak against Orcish Bowmasters?
output: Elves runs almost entirely 1-toughness creatures: Deathrite Shaman, Heritage Druid, Quirion Ranger, Birchlore Rangers, Elvish Visionary. Bowmasters' enter-the-battlefield trigger deals 1 damage and creates a 1/1, instantly killing any of these. Additionally, every cantrip and draw effect (Glimpse of Nature draws) triggers Bowmasters again. An Elves player casting Glimpse and chaining 5 creatures draws 5 cards, triggering Bowmasters 5 times, killing 5 elves and growing the Army to a 6/6. Bowmasters single-handedly dismantles the Elves engine.

instruction: Why does Sneak and Show struggle against Karakas?
output: Karakas returns target legendary creature to its owner's hand for free (tap Karakas). Emrakul, the Aeons Torn is legendary. After you Sneak Attack or Show and Tell Emrakul into play, the opponent can simply tap Karakas to bounce it back to your hand. The annihilator trigger still happens when Emrakul attacks (if they wait until attackers are declared), but they can Karakas before the declare attackers step to prevent even that. Atraxa, Grand Unifier is also legendary. This is why 21% of Legacy decks play Karakas.

instruction: Why does Red Stompy struggle against Force of Vigor?
output: Red Stompy relies on turn-1 lock pieces like Blood Moon, Chalice of the Void, and Ensnaring Bridge. Force of Vigor destroys two artifacts and/or enchantments for free by pitching a green card. A single Force of Vigor can destroy both Chalice AND Blood Moon in one shot, completely dismantling the prison at zero mana. Green decks boarding in 2-3 Force of Vigor make Stompy's lock pieces unreliable, and without the lock, Stompy's threats are mediocre.

instruction: How do I build a Legacy deck from scratch?
output: Step 1: Choose a strategy (tempo, control, combo, prison, midrange). Step 2: Select your core engine cards (Brainstorm + Force for blue, Chalice + fast mana for prison, rituals + wincon for combo). Step 3: Build the mana base (14+ sources of primary color, fetchlands, 2-4 basics). Step 4: Fill with the most efficient cards for your strategy at each mana cost. Step 5: Build a 15-card sideboard covering the top 5-8 matchups. Step 6: Goldfish 20+ hands to check consistency. Step 7: Test against tier 1 decks. Step 8: Iterate based on results. Most importantly: study existing successful decklists before brewing from zero.

instruction: What is the most important card slot in a Legacy deck?
output: The 1-mana spell slot. Legacy is defined by 1-mana spells: Brainstorm, Ponder, Thoughtseize, Swords to Plowshares, Lightning Bolt, Daze (free but MV 1), Delver of Secrets, Dark Ritual, Goblin Lackey, Mother of Runes, Aether Vial. Every competitive deck maximizes its 1-mana plays. If your deck's best turn-1 play is passing with a land, you are probably too slow for Legacy. The exception is prison decks that use 0-mana acceleration (Ancient Tomb, Lotus Petal) to deploy 2-3 mana lock pieces on turn 1 instead.

instruction: How do I know if my Legacy deck is good?
output: Test against the tier 1 metagame: 1) Does it have a positive win rate against Dimir Tempo (14.6%)? If not, you lose your most common matchup. 2) Does it have a plan against fast combo (Oops, Storm)? If not, you get turn-1/2 killed. 3) Does it function through Chalice of the Void on 1? If not, prison decks destroy you. 4) Can it win by turn 5-6 against a goldfish? If not, you are too slow. 5) Is every card in your deck the most efficient version of its effect? If you are playing 2+ mana cards that could be 1-mana alternatives, replace them. A Legacy deck that passes all 5 tests is competitive.

instruction: What is wrong with playing more than 60 cards in Legacy?
output: Every card above 60 reduces your probability of drawing your best cards. If your best card is a 4-of in 60 cards, you see it in about 40% of opening hands. In 65 cards, that drops to 37%. In 80 cards (Yorion), it drops to 32%. Legacy rewards consistency because matchups are diverse and you need specific answers. The only exception is Yorion companion (80 cards), where the guaranteed companion value compensates for reduced consistency. Even then, Yorion builds accept lower consistency as a trade-off.

instruction: When should I splash a third color in my Legacy deck?
output: Splash a third color only when: 1) The splashed cards are worth the mana base damage (Uro in Bant Control, Pyroblast in Grixis). 2) You can support it with 1-2 dual lands and fetchlands that already serve your main colors. 3) The splash does not weaken your Blood Moon resilience (more nonbasics = more vulnerability). 4) You have enough cantrips to find the splash color when needed. Bad reasons to splash: adding a marginally better removal spell, or a creature that is only slightly better than alternatives in your main colors. The deckbuilding cost of a splash (life from fetching, Wasteland vulnerability, Blood Moon risk) must be outweighed by the power gain.

instruction: Why does every Delver variant play exactly 4 Brainstorm and 4 Ponder?
output: Brainstorm and Ponder are the foundation of every blue Legacy deck. They provide: consistency (find the right answer for any matchup), Delver flip setup (put an instant/sorcery on top), graveyard fueling (fetchland cracks after Brainstorm fill the graveyard for Murktide delve), and protection setup (find Force of Will when needed). Playing fewer than 4 of either reduces the deck's most powerful advantage: seeing more cards than the opponent. There is no reasonable argument for cutting cantrips in a Delver deck.

instruction: Why does Lands play Maze of Ith?
output: Maze of Ith untaps an attacking creature and prevents all combat damage it deals and receives. In Lands, this serves as free repeatable creature removal that does not cost a card or mana. Against Delver's Murktide Regent, Maze neutralizes it every turn indefinitely. The self-targeting trick (attack with Knight of the Reliquary, Maze your own Knight) effectively gives your creatures vigilance. Maze is findable by Crop Rotation at instant speed.

instruction: Why does Storm play Ponder over Preordain as the second cantrip?
output: Ponder sees 3 cards and can shuffle if all 3 are bad, effectively seeing 4 cards with a fetchland. In Storm, you are looking for very specific combo pieces (LED, Burning Wish, rituals) and the ability to shuffle away 3 blanks is critical. Ponder also sets up the top of your library for Ad Nauseam (you want low-cost cards on top). Storm needs to find its pieces by turn 2-3, and Ponder's deeper card selection achieves this better than Preordain's scry 2.

instruction: Why do some Dimir Tempo lists play Snuff Out?
output: Snuff Out costs 4 life to cast for free if you control a Swamp (Underground Sea counts). This gives Dimir Tempo a zero-mana removal spell, allowing you to deploy threats AND remove opposing creatures in the same turn. It is particularly strong against Murktide Regent (which dodges Lightning Bolt). The 4-life cost is significant against aggressive decks but acceptable in matchups where board control matters more than life total. Most lists play 1-2 copies.

instruction: Why do prison decks play fast mana but tempo decks do not?
output: Prison decks need to deploy lock pieces (Chalice, Blood Moon, Trinisphere) on turn 1 to be effective. A turn-2 Blood Moon is dramatically weaker because the opponent has already fetched their basics. Fast mana (Ancient Tomb, City of Traitors, Lotus Petal, Chrome Mox) enables these critical turn-1 plays. Tempo decks do not need fast mana because their spells cost 0-2 mana naturally, and fast mana does not help cast cantrips or Daze more efficiently. Tempo decks want colored mana sources, not colorless acceleration.

instruction: Why does Death and Taxes play a high number of utility lands?
output: D&T leverages Aether Vial for creature deployment, freeing its mana for land abilities. This means lands like Rishadan Port (tap opponent's land), Wasteland (destroy nonbasic), Karakas (bounce legends), Mishra's Factory (2/2 creature land), and Flagstones of Trokair (fetch Plains when destroyed) all provide value without competing with creature casting. No other Legacy deck can play 8+ utility lands because no other deck has Vial reducing the need for mana from lands.

instruction: Why do most Legacy sideboards include exactly 15 cards?
output: The maximum sideboard size is 15 cards. Playing fewer than 15 is almost always wrong because you are giving up free options. Each sideboard slot should improve at least one matchup by being better than the weakest maindeck card in that matchup. Even if your last few slots are marginal, they are still better than having no option. The only argument for fewer than 15: if you literally cannot think of a card that improves any matchup, which is essentially impossible given Legacy's card pool.

instruction: Why does every Legacy deck run exactly 60 cards in the main?
output: Every card above 60 reduces your probability of drawing your best cards. Mathematical proof: with a 4-of in 60 cards, you see it in your opening 7 with ~40% probability. At 61 cards, it drops slightly. At 65, meaningfully. The cumulative effect across all your key cards (Force of Will, Brainstorm, your best threat, your best answer) is a significant consistency reduction. Legacy rewards consistency because you need specific cards for specific matchups. The only exception is Yorion companion (80 cards), where the guaranteed companion justifies the cost.

instruction: What makes Wasteland better in some decks than others?
output: Wasteland is best in decks that: 1) Operate on fewer lands (Delver at 18-19 lands can afford to sacrifice one; Control at 25 cannot). 2) Have mana denial synergy (Daze + Wasteland is devastating when combined). 3) Run cheap spells that function after Wasting their own land. 4) Do not need every land to produce colored mana (colorless from Wasteland is acceptable when your spells cost 1). Wasteland is worst in: control decks that need every land drop, combo decks that need colored mana for their combo, and 3+ color decks where every land needs to fix mana.

instruction: How many win conditions does a control deck need?
output: Typically 2-6, depending on how redundant they are. Jeskai Control might run 2 Monastery Mentor and 1-2 Entreat the Angels - enough to find one via cantrips. Beanstalk Control wins through accumulated value from 4 Up the Beanstalk and does not need dedicated win conditions. Lands wins with 2 Dark Depths + 2 Thespian's Stage (the combo IS the win condition). The key: cantrips let you find a single win condition reliably, so you do not need many. Every slot spent on a win condition is a slot not spent on answers.

instruction: Why do some Legacy decks play Mishra's Bauble?
output: Mishra's Bauble costs 0 mana and draws a card at the beginning of the next upkeep (delayed draw). It fills multiple roles: 1) Free artifact for Urza's Saga Constructs and metalcraft. 2) Contributes to delirium (artifact type in graveyard) for Dragon's Rage Channeler. 3) Fuels delve for Murktide Regent. 4) Lets you see the top card of any library (information). 5) It is a free spell for storm count. The card does almost nothing individually, but its zero cost and synergies make it powerful in specific shells.

instruction: Why does every Legacy combo deck play some form of protection?
output: Force of Will is in 45% of Legacy decks. If your combo resolves, you win, but 45% of the time your opponent has a free counterspell ready. Without protection, your combo is countered almost half the time. Protection options: Force of Will (counter their Force), Pact of Negation (free counter on combo turn), Veil of Summer (stops blue and black interaction for G), Thoughtseize (strip their Force before comboing), Silence (prevent all spells for W), Defense Grid (spells cost 3 more on opponent's turn). Every competitive combo deck runs 4-8 protection pieces.

instruction: What is a transformational sideboard and when should I use one?
output: A transformational sideboard changes your deck's strategy entirely post-board. Example: Doomsday bringing in a Show and Tell package so opponents who sided in graveyard hate face a completely different combo. Storm bringing in a creature beatdown plan (Young Pyromancer, Monastery Mentor) so opponents with anti-storm hate (Mindbreak Trap, Deafening Silence) face irrelevant sideboard cards. Transformational sideboards work best when: 1) your game 1 strategy has a specific hate card that blanks it, 2) the transformation dodges that hate entirely, and 3) you have enough sideboard slots (usually 8-10). The risk is inconsistency from having two half-strategies.

instruction: Why do Legacy Delver decks play fewer lands than other formats?
output: Legacy Delver decks typically run 18-19 lands compared to 22-24 in Modern or Standard. This works because: 1) 8 cantrips (Brainstorm + Ponder) let you keep 1-land hands and find the second land reliably. 2) Spells cost 0-2 mana, so you rarely need more than 3 lands in play. 3) Daze returns a land to hand, effectively letting you 'replay' it. 4) Wasteland is a land that you WANT to sacrifice. Playing fewer lands maximizes spell density, which means more threats and interaction per game. The cantrip engine compensates for the low land count.

instruction: How do I choose between main-decking removal vs counterspells?
output: Consider your strategy and the metagame: 1) Tempo decks: prioritize counterspells (Daze, Force of Will) over removal because you want to protect your threats and deny opponent's plays. Run 2-4 removal as flex. 2) Control decks: balance both - you need removal for resolved threats and counterspells for stack interaction. 3) Creature matchups: removal is better (Swords to Plowshares, Lightning Bolt). 4) Combo matchups: counterspells are better (Force of Will, Daze). Since Legacy is roughly 40% combo, having 4 Force of Will mainboard is almost mandatory for blue decks. Then add removal based on how many creatures you expect to face.

instruction: What is the role of Preordain vs Consider in Legacy?
output: Both are cantrips that cost U. Preordain scrys 2 then draws 1 (see 3 cards, can bottom 2). Consider surveys 1 (look at top card, may put in graveyard) then draws 1 (see 2 cards, can mill 1). Preordain is better for card SELECTION (see more cards, choose better). Consider is better for GRAVEYARD FUELING (instant speed, puts a card in graveyard for Murktide delve, delirium, or Cabal Therapy). In Dimir Tempo, Consider is sometimes preferred because the instant speed and graveyard synergy outweigh Preordain's deeper selection. In combo, Preordain is always better.

instruction: Why do some decks play a single copy of a card?
output: One-ofs in Legacy serve specific purposes: 1) Tutor targets: Green Sun's Zenith decks play 1-of creatures for each situation (1 Collector Ouphe, 1 Endurance, 1 Dryad Arbor). 2) Legendary cards: Karakas is legendary, so multiples are dead. Run 1. 3) Sideboard tech: 1-of Massacre in sideboard is enough when you can find it with cantrips. 4) Wish targets: Burning Wish's sideboard has 1-ofs for each situation. The principle: in a cantrip-heavy format, 1-ofs are effectively more than 1 copy because Brainstorm and Ponder find them.

instruction: How do I decide between playing aggressive or controlling with a tempo deck?
output: The decision depends on your role in each matchup: 1) Against combo: be aggressive. Deploy a threat early and protect it with countermagic. You are the clock. 2) Against aggro: be controlling. Remove their threats, stabilize, then deploy your own. 3) Against control: be aggressive early (under their shields) then transition to protecting what you have. 4) Against prison: depends on their lock. If Chalice is on 1, you cannot cantrip - be aggressive with existing threats. If Blood Moon is out, adapt your mana usage. The hallmark of a skilled tempo pilot is recognizing your role and adjusting mid-game.

instruction: Why does Dimir Tempo play Undercity Sewers?
output: Undercity Sewers is a surveil land that enters tapped and surveys 1 (look at top card, may put in graveyard) when it enters. In Dimir Tempo, the surveil fuels Murktide Regent delve and provides card selection. It is typically a 1-of that you fetch with Polluted Delta when you do not need an untapped land this turn. The tapped entry is a real cost, so you only fetch it when you can afford to lose a turn of tempo.

instruction: Why does Sneak and Show not play Daze?
output: Sneak and Show needs to reach 3-4 mana to combo (Show and Tell costs 2U, Sneak Attack costs 3R). Daze requires returning an Island, which sets you back a land drop and delays the combo by a turn. The deck already plays Force of Will for free protection, and its mana needs (Ancient Tomb, Lotus Petal) are optimized for reaching combo mana fast. Daze would be anti-synergistic: you want MORE mana, not less. Sneak and Show plays defensive countermagic (Force, Flusterstorm) instead of tempo-positive countermagic (Daze).

instruction: Why does Painter play Imperial Recruiter?
output: Imperial Recruiter is a 1/1 for 2R that tutors any creature with power 2 or less to your hand. In Painter, this finds: Painter's Servant (1/3, the combo piece), Goblin Welder (1/1, artifact recursion), Goblin Engineer (1/1, artifact tutor), and Magus of the Moon (2/2, Blood Moon on a body). Recruiter turns every creature in the deck into a toolbox target. The 2R cost is manageable with Ancient Tomb. It is a critical consistency piece that finds whichever half of the combo you are missing.

instruction: Why do some Dimir Tempo lists play Barrowgoyf over Tarmogoyf?
output: Barrowgoyf from MH3 counts card types in your graveyard only, not both graveyards. This is better with Leyline of the Void (which exiles opponent's cards, shrinking their graveyard and thus old Tarmogoyf). Barrowgoyf also has a self-mill ability that fuels its own growth and Murktide's delve. In the current meta where 36% of decks play Leyline, Barrowgoyf is more reliable than Tarmogoyf. The trade-off: Barrowgoyf cannot grow from opponent's card types, making it slightly smaller in games without graveyard hate.

instruction: Why does Eldrazi play Thought-Knot Seer over other 4-drops?
output: Thought-Knot Seer is a 4/4 for 3C (3 colorless + 1 colorless from Eldrazi Temple = castable turn 2) that exiles a card from the opponent's hand on ETB. This combines disruption and a threat in one card. The hand exile is better than Thoughtseize in some ways: it exiles (no graveyard interactions) and takes any nonland card. The 'draw a card when it leaves' downside is acceptable because the 4/4 body often trades favorably in combat. No other 4-drop provides this combination of disruption and clock for a colorless deck.

instruction: Why does Burn play Price of Progress?
output: Price of Progress deals 2 damage per nonbasic land the opponent controls. In Legacy, most decks run 15-18 nonbasic lands. Against a typical Dimir Tempo mana base with 3 Underground Sea, 4 Wasteland, and 4 fetchlands in play (some cracked), Price of Progress often deals 6-10 damage for just 1R. This is the most efficient burn spell in Legacy against multi-color decks. Against mono-color decks with many basics (Burn mirror, Red Stompy), Price is much weaker. It is why budget Legacy mana bases with shock lands are especially vulnerable to Burn.

instruction: Why does D&T play Skyclave Apparition?
output: Skyclave Apparition is a 2/2 for 1WW that exiles a nonland, nontoken permanent with mana value 4 or less when it enters. In Legacy, this hits almost everything: Murktide Regent (MV 7... wait, Murktide has MV 7 but delve reduces the COST, not the mana value. Murktide has MV 7, so Apparition cannot exile it). It DOES hit: Orcish Bowmasters (MV 2), Delver (MV 1), Painter's Servant (MV 2), Chalice of the Void (MV 0 on battlefield), Up the Beanstalk (MV 2). When Apparition leaves, opponent gets a token. Flickerwisp can exile Apparition to reset the trigger, permanently exiling the target.

instruction: Why do Legacy decks rarely play creatures that cost 4 or more mana?
output: Legacy's speed demands mana efficiency. By turn 4, combo decks have already won or been stopped, tempo decks have a threat + multiple disruption pieces deployed, and prison decks have 2-3 lock pieces in play. A 4-mana creature that just attacks is too slow. The exceptions prove the rule: Murktide Regent costs UU with delve (effectively 2 mana), Emrakul is cheated into play (never cast for 15), Thought-Knot Seer disrupts on ETB, and Karn the Great Creator is a toolbox. Every 4+ mana card in Legacy must either be cheated into play or provide game-altering value on ETB.

instruction: How do you decide between Thoughtseize and Cabal Therapy?
output: Thoughtseize costs B and lets you see the opponent's hand and take any nonland card. Guaranteed information and targeted disruption. Cabal Therapy costs B and you NAME a card without seeing their hand. If correct, they discard all copies. It has flashback by sacrificing a creature. Thoughtseize is better when: you do not know what the opponent has, you need reliable disruption, and you have no sacrifice fodder. Cabal Therapy is better when: you have seen their hand (from Probe or prior Thoughtseize), you have expendable creatures (Elves, Veteran Explorer in Nic Fit), and you want to strip multiple copies. Most decks play Thoughtseize for reliability.

instruction: How do I evaluate whether to include a new card in my Legacy deck?
output: Ask these questions: 1) Is it more efficient than what it replaces? Card must do the same job better or do a unique job that justifies the slot. 2) Does it survive the format's interaction? Creatures need 3+ toughness (Bolt test, Bowmasters test) or provide value on ETB. 3) Does it work with your deck's engine? In cantrip decks, cards should be blue (for Force of Will pitch). In Chalice decks, cards should not cost the Chalice number. 4) Is it good in multiple matchups? Narrow cards belong in sideboards. Maindeck cards should be useful in 60%+ of games. 5) What do you cut? Every addition requires a cut. If you cannot identify a card that is worse, the new card probably does not make it.

instruction: Why do competitive Legacy players rarely change more than 2-3 cards between tournaments?
output: Legacy decks are highly optimized over years of play. Each card slot has been tested and justified. Changing more than 2-3 cards at once makes it impossible to tell which change helped or hurt. Competitive players: 1) Change 1-2 mainboard flex slots based on expected metagame (swap removal spells, adjust threat mix). 2) Change 2-3 sideboard slots based on what they expect to face. 3) Keep the core 50-52 mainboard cards and 10-12 sideboard cards stable. The disciplined approach means you learn your deck deeply and attribute wins/losses to play decisions rather than deck construction variance.

instruction: What is the ideal ratio of threats to interaction in a Legacy deck?
output: Depends on strategy: Tempo (Dimir Delver): ~12 threats, ~16 interaction, ~8 cantrips, ~19 lands. You want enough threats to pressure but mostly disruption. Control (Beanstalk): ~4-8 win conditions, ~20 interaction, ~8 cantrips, ~24 lands. Win conditions are sparse because cantrips find them. Combo (Storm): ~6-8 combo pieces, ~8 protection, ~12 cantrips + rituals, ~15 lands. Most of the deck IS the combo. Prison (Red Stompy): ~8-12 threats, ~8-12 lock pieces, ~8 fast mana, ~20 lands. Lock pieces are both threats and interaction. The common thread: cantrips let you run fewer of any category because you find what you need.

instruction: What makes a good sideboard card in Legacy?
output: The best sideboard cards share these qualities: 1) High impact when drawn: Leyline of the Void single-handedly wins the graveyard matchup. 2) Broad applicability: Pyroblast hits Murktide AND counters Force AND kills Jace - useful against multiple blue decks. 3) Low mana cost: you need to deploy hate before the opponent executes their plan. Free cards (Leyline, Force of Vigor, Mindbreak Trap) are premium. 4) Does not hurt your own strategy: Null Rod in a deck with artifact mana is a nonbo. Leyline in a Murktide deck exiles your own graveyard... wait, Leyline only affects opponents. 5) Not already mainboard: if a card is good enough for maindeck, it should be there. Sideboard cards are specifically for targeted matchups where they dramatically improve your win rate.

instruction: When should I play Flusterstorm vs Spell Pierce?
output: Flusterstorm (U): counters target instant or sorcery unless its controller pays 1 for each copy (storm copies = your storm count). Best against: Storm (your Flusterstorm has storm too, creating enough copies to counter every Storm spell), counter wars (multiple copies counter multiple countermagic), and any stack-heavy matchup. Spell Pierce (U): counters target noncreature spell unless they pay 2. Best against: fair decks where opponents tap low (they often cannot pay 2 early), any noncreature spell. Choose Flusterstorm when: you expect Storm or counter-heavy matchups. Choose Spell Pierce when: you want to catch planeswalkers, enchantments (Up the Beanstalk), and sorceries (Show and Tell). Many players run a split for flexibility.

instruction: Why do some Legacy players run a split of 2 Pyroblast and 2 Red Elemental Blast?
output: Functionally they are almost identical: both cost R to counter a blue spell or destroy a blue permanent. The split is to play around two cards: 1) Meddling Mage: if opponent names Pyroblast, your Red Elemental Blasts still work (different card names). 2) Surgical Extraction: if opponent Surgicals your Pyroblast, your REBs survive. 3) Cabal Therapy: if named, only half your copies are hit. The strategic diversification costs nothing (both cards do the same thing) but protects against narrow hate that names specific cards. This is a Legacy-specific optimization that reflects the format's attention to detail.

instruction: What is a 'cantrip' in Legacy and why are they so important?
output: A cantrip is a spell that replaces itself by drawing a card (or providing card selection). Legacy cantrips: Brainstorm (U, draw 3 put back 2), Ponder (U, look at 3 with shuffle option), Preordain (U, scry 2 draw 1), Consider (U, surveil 1 draw 1). Cantrips are the backbone of blue Legacy because they: 1) Find specific answers (Surgical vs combo, Swords vs creatures). 2) Smooth draws (keep 1-land hands and find the second). 3) Fuel Delver flips, delirium, and delve. 4) Provide shuffle effects with fetchlands. 5) Cost only 1 mana, fitting into any turn. Without cantrips, Legacy decks would be at the mercy of random draws. With them, blue decks effectively play with a 40-card deck because they see so many more cards.
